% Chapter 1: Introduction — generated by ResearcherAgent + WriterAgent + LaTeXAgent
\chapter{Introduction to Multi-Agent Orchestration}
\label{ch:intro}

Multi-agent systems represent a paradigm shift in artificial intelligence, moving from
single monolithic models to collaborative networks of specialised agents. This article
examines the architectural patterns, frameworks, and production challenges that define
modern multi-agent orchestration.

The field has evolved rapidly since the introduction of large language models (LLMs) as
agent cores \parencite{LangChain2023}. Today, frameworks such as CrewAI \parencite{CrewAI2024},
LangGraph \parencite{LangGraph2024}, and AutoGen \parencite{AutoGen2023} provide scaffolding for
building production-grade agent teams.

  \label{fig:crew-architecture}

\section{Scope and Objectives}

This article pursues three objectives:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item Survey canonical agent architectures and their trade-offs (Chapter~\ref{ch:arch}).
  \item Compare leading orchestration frameworks (Chapter~\ref{ch:frameworks}).
  \item Analyse production deployment patterns and challenges (Chapter~\ref{ch:production}).
\end{enumerate}

The case study in Chapter~\ref{ch:casestudy} demonstrates the pipeline that produced this
very article, closing the loop between theory and practice.

\section{Structure of This Article}

Chapter~\ref{ch:arch} covers agent topologies from single-agent loops to hierarchical
multi-agent networks. Chapter~\ref{ch:frameworks} provides a comparative analysis of three
major frameworks. Chapter~\ref{ch:production} addresses rate limiting, observability, and
fault tolerance. Chapter~\ref{ch:bidi} demonstrates bilingual (Hebrew–English) content
rendering under LuaLaTeX with \texttt{polyglossia}. Chapter~\ref{ch:casestudy} presents
the end-to-end case study.
